Difference Plots

Goal

This notebook compares paired estimates, then plots the calculated gap directly.

The main figure we will use is the Cleveland-style dot plot: categories on one axis, values on a shared quantitative scale, and dots rather than bars.

Analytic Questions

  • How do PRAMS anxiety estimates differ from depression estimates?
  • Which states have larger anxiety-minus-depression gaps?
  • Why can a dot plot be clearer than paired bars?
  • When should we plot the difference directly?

Load Data

prams <- readr::read_csv("data/real/prams_2011_selected.csv")
Rows: 1222 Columns: 8
── Column specification ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────
Delimiter: ","
chr (4): location_abbr, location, subgroup_cat, subgroup
dbl (4): depression_within_3_months_birth, anxiety_within_3_months_birth, bi...

ℹ Use `spec()` to retrieve the full column specification for this data.
ℹ Specify the column types or set `show_col_types = FALSE` to quiet this message.

Prepare Data

PRAMS values are already aggregated percentages for state/subgroup rows.

In this dataset, subgroup_cat == "None" marks the overall state row.

Only some states have both depression and anxiety estimates in this teaching file, so we keep the rows where both are available.

Data preparation step Functions in this chunk
Keep or remove rows filter()
Create or change columns transmute()
Sort rows arrange() 
state_mental_health <- prams |>
  filter(
    subgroup_cat == "None",
    !is.na(depression_within_3_months_birth),
    !is.na(anxiety_within_3_months_birth)
  ) |>
  transmute(
    location,
    depression = depression_within_3_months_birth,
    anxiety = anxiety_within_3_months_birth,
    difference = anxiety - depression
  ) |>
  arrange(difference)

state_mental_health
# A tibble: 10 × 4
   location      depression anxiety difference
   <chr>              <dbl>   <dbl>      <dbl>
 1 Wyoming             12.6    10.9     -1.7  
 2 Utah                12.5    12.2     -0.300
 3 Maryland            11.4    11.6      0.200
 4 Michigan            14.3    15.2      0.900
 5 West Virginia       15.5    16.6      1.10 
 6 Hawaii               4.2     5.6      1.4  
 7 Minnesota           10.5    12        1.5  
 8 Missouri            12      14.1      2.1  
 9 Delaware            11.5    14.3      2.8  
10 Wisconsin           13.3    16.2      2.9

The difference is measured in percentage points.

  • Positive: anxiety estimate is higher than depression estimate.
  • Negative: anxiety estimate is lower than depression estimate.
  • Zero: the two estimates are equal.

Paired Estimates As Dots

First, compare the two estimates directly.

A Cleveland dot plot uses position on a common scale. This is usually easier to compare than paired bars.

Data preparation step Functions in this chunk
Choose or rename columns select()
Create or change columns mutate()
Reshape data pivot_longer()
Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings x; y; color
Geoms / stats geom_point()
geom_point() properties size
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels labs(); theme_minimal() 
paired_estimates <- state_mental_health |>
  select(location, depression, anxiety) |>
  tidyr::pivot_longer(
    cols = c(depression, anxiety),
    names_to = "measure",
    values_to = "estimate"
  ) |>
  mutate(
    measure = recode(
      measure,
      depression = "Depression",
      anxiety = "Anxiety"
    ),
    location = factor(location, levels = state_mental_health$location)
  )

ggplot(
  paired_estimates,
  aes(x = estimate, y = location, color = measure)
) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  labs(
    title = "PRAMS Mental Health Estimates By State",
    x = "Estimate (%)",
    y = NULL,
    color = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

This plot shows both estimates, but the gap still has to be judged by eye.

Connected Dot Plot

A connected dot plot makes the paired structure more visible.

Data preparation step Functions in this chunk
Create or change columns mutate()
Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings y; x; xend; yend; color
Geoms / stats geom_segment(); geom_point()
geom_segment() properties color; linewidth
geom_point() properties (layer 1) size
geom_point() properties (layer 2) size
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels scale_color_manual(); labs(); theme_minimal() 
paired_wide <- state_mental_health |>
  mutate(location = factor(location, levels = state_mental_health$location))

ggplot(paired_wide, aes(y = location)) +
  geom_segment(
    aes(x = depression, xend = anxiety, yend = location),
    color = "gray70",
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  geom_point(aes(x = depression, color = "Depression"), size = 3) +
  geom_point(aes(x = anxiety, color = "Anxiety"), size = 3) +
  scale_color_manual(
    values = c("Depression" = "gray35", "Anxiety" = "steelblue"),
    name = NULL
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "Anxiety And Depression Estimates Are Paired Within State",
    x = "Estimate (%)",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

This is useful when the reader needs to see both original values and the paired comparison.

Not here how we specified colors with the named vector c("Depression" = "gray35") and directly referenced the color in geom_point. This is a nice expressive technique.

Plot The Difference Directly

If the question is about the gap, calculate and plot the gap.

Below we’ll use reorder() to make reorder the factor categories by location and the size of the difference.

Data preparation step Functions in this chunk
Create or change columns mutate()
Plot component Code in this chunk
Aesthetic mappings x; y; xend; yend
Geoms / stats geom_vline(); geom_segment(); geom_point()
geom_vline() properties color
geom_segment() properties color
geom_point() properties size; color
Scales / coordinates / facets / labels labs(); theme_minimal() 
difference_data <- state_mental_health |>
  mutate(location = reorder(location, difference))

ggplot(difference_data, aes(x = difference, y = location)) +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 0, color = "gray60") +
  geom_segment(
    aes(x = 0, xend = difference, yend = location),
    color = "gray70"
  ) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "steelblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Gap Between Anxiety And Depression Estimates",
    x = "Anxiety minus depression, percentage points",
    y = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

Most states shown have anxiety estimates slightly higher than depression estimates. Wyoming and Utah are below zero, meaning the depression estimate is higher than the anxiety estimate in those state overall rows.

Difference Calculations

The plotted value is not one of the original columns. We derive it ourselves with dplyr.

state_mental_health |>
  select(location, anxiety, depression, difference)
# A tibble: 10 × 4
   location      anxiety depression difference
   <chr>           <dbl>      <dbl>      <dbl>
 1 Wyoming          10.9       12.6     -1.7  
 2 Utah             12.2       12.5     -0.300
 3 Maryland         11.6       11.4      0.200
 4 Michigan         15.2       14.3      0.900
 5 West Virginia    16.6       15.5      1.10 
 6 Hawaii            5.6        4.2      1.4  
 7 Minnesota        12         10.5      1.5  
 8 Missouri         14.1       12        2.1  
 9 Delaware         14.3       11.5      2.8  
10 Wisconsin        16.2       13.3      2.9

That derived quantity should have clear units and direction.

Here:

  • Unit: percentage points.
  • Direction: anxiety estimate minus depression estimate.
  • Zero: no gap between the two estimates.

Takeaways

  • Cleveland-style dot plots use position on a common scale and avoid unnecessary bar area.
  • Connected dot plots are useful when paired original values still matter.
  • If the question is about a difference, plot the difference directly.
  • Always label whether differences are percentage points, raw units, ratios, or percent changes.
